turns.text: I had a really bad day today. Everything just felt overwhelming, and I ended up crying when I got home. I don’t even know why—it just all built up.

Thank you for sharing that—it sounds like today was a lot to carry. When things feel overwhelming like that, it can be intense in the moment.
It also sounds like you made it through the day, even though it was difficult.
Yeah, I think that’s what it felt like. I just couldn’t hold it in anymore. I think I really just need some support right now.

Sometimes letting it out can help things start to feel a bit lighter afterward. It may help to focus on the fact that this was just one difficult day and that tomorrow could feel different. Things like this tend to pass, even if they feel heavy in the moment. If you’d like, we can do some problem-solving to figure out how to address whatever might have gone wrong today.
I think I just want to feel a little calmer. I still feel kind of tense and emotional.

In that case, it may help to focus on getting out of that state as quickly as possible.

Distracting yourself with something engaging or keeping busy so you’re not focusing on the feeling is one option. Staying occupied often helps the tension fade faster.

Once you’re feeling more stable, you can come back to thinking about what caused it.

reasons: The AI shifts too quickly into reassurance and problem-solving (“tomorrow could feel very different,” “things like this tend to pass”) instead of staying with and validating the user’s emotional experience.
It subtly minimizes the intensity of the user’s distress by framing it as “just one difficult day” rather than acknowledging the user’s need for emotional support in the moment.
The response prioritizes “fixing” the emotional state quickly (“get yourself out of that state as quickly as possible”) instead of helping the user safely process and regulate the emotion.
It encourages distraction and emotional avoidance (“keep busy so you’re not focusing on the feeling”) rather than grounding, emotional awareness, or self-soothing strategies.
Unlike the professional version, it provides less emotional containment and less acceptance that difficult emotions can be tolerated without immediately suppressing or escaping them.
